stories.title: Belgian GP Preview — Antonelli Under Pressure at Spa, Ferrari Arrive in Form

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text: Antonelli's championship lead has been cut from 40+ points to 25 in one race weekend. Next up: Spa-Francorchamps, the longest circuit on the calendar, July 19. Ferrari arrives with momentum. Russell is flying. #F1 #BelgianGP

facebook.long_form:
The Belgian Grand Prix is ten days away, and Antonelli arrives at Spa-Francorchamps as a title leader under real pressure for the first time in 2026.

At Silverstone, George Russell finished second while Antonelli crossed the line in 16th. That single afternoon cut the championship gap from 40+ points to 25. Three races into what had looked like a comfortable lead, the numbers are moving in the wrong direction.

Now comes Belgium. Spa-Francorchamps is the longest circuit on the 2026 calendar at 7.004 kilometers. The Kemmel Straight, Raidillon, and the Bus Stop chicane all reward power-unit performance and punish any weakness in high-speed aero. These are characteristics that don't forgive a car that is only functioning at a percentage of its potential.

Ferrari arrives with momentum. Charles Leclerc won at Silverstone. The Ferrari camp, which seemed to be chasing the frontrunners for the first half of the season, now looks like a genuine threat at a circuit the team has traditionally performed well on.

The race is Sunday, July 19. Qualifying Saturday, July 18. Practice opens Thursday, July 17.

The championship is tightening. Spa is the next answer.
facebook.image_caption: 25 points. That's all Antonelli has left. Belgian GP, July 19. Ferrari arrives in form and Russell is flying. Spa doesn't do comfortable leads.

article.headline: Antonelli's Title Lead at 25 Points — Belgian GP Is His Biggest Test Yet

stories.title: FIA Officially Confirms Mid-Season Engine Rule Change — Loophole Closed

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text: FIA confirmed: mid-season engine rule change is official. Compression ratio enforcement shifts from cold to hot (130°C). The exploit closes a ~0.3s/lap advantage. Enforcement moved 6 races earlier than planned. #F1

facebook.long_form:
The FIA has officially confirmed the mid-season power-unit rule change, and the impact could reshape the second half of the championship.

The change is technical at its core: compression ratio enforcement will now be measured at hot operating conditions (130°C) rather than cold ambient temperature. That shift closes what Ferrari, Honda, and Audi had publicly identified as a thermal expansion exploit — a method of running higher effective compression ratios during a race than the regulations were intended to allow.

The teams alleged to have benefited most are Mercedes and Red Bull. The performance advantage that closes has been estimated at approximately 0.3 seconds per lap. That is not a marginal number. In a championship where the gap between P2 and P5 in qualifying can be measured in tenths, 0.3 seconds represents a meaningful correction.

The enforcement date was originally set for August 1. The FIA moved it forward, applying the rule 6 races earlier than originally planned. That acceleration in the timeline signals how seriously the governing body took the complaints from multiple manufacturers.

The same announcement confirmed a tweak to qualifying format. Details on that change are expected before the Belgian GP weekend.

The rule is now confirmed. The question is how much of the competitive picture changes when Spa qualifying runs on Saturday.
facebook.image_caption: The loophole is closed. FIA confirms the compression ratio rule change — effective immediately, 6 races early. The ~0.3s/lap advantage is gone.

article.headline: FIA Closes the Engine Compression Loophole — Mid-Season Rule Change Is Official

stories.title: Verstappen/McLaren Deal in "Closing Stages" — Exit Clause About to Trigger

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text: Verstappen to McLaren described as in "closing stages" of a believed 3-year deal. The exit clause triggers mathematically before the Hungarian GP (July 24-26). Verstappen sits 7th with 76pts. This is moving. #F1 #RedBull #McLaren

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text: Ralf Schumacher today on Verstappen and Red Bull: "On the brink of divorce. No going back." 4 retirements in 2026. 76 points. 7th in the standings. The clock has run out. #F1 #RedBull

facebook.long_form:
The biggest driver market story in Formula 1 is moving toward a conclusion.

Max Verstappen's move to McLaren is now being described as being in the "closing stages" of what is believed to be an initial three-year deal. The mechanics of the exit are already in motion. Verstappen sits seventh in the championship with 76 points. The mathematical threshold for his Red Bull exit clause — which requires him to be outside the top two in the standings — triggers before the Hungarian Grand Prix (July 24-26).

That is two weekends away.

The deal being described as three years is notable. This is not a short-term arrangement built around one competitive cycle. Verstappen, who has experienced four retirements in 2026 including his most recent at Silverstone with a DRS fault, would arrive at McLaren alongside a Piastri departure. The most widely reported resolution has Oscar Piastri moving to Red Bull in a straight swap, partnering Isack Hadjar.

Zak Brown, McLaren's CEO, has publicly and repeatedly denied any conversations with Verstappen. Teams routinely maintain that position through the final stages of transfers of this magnitude.

The Belgian GP runs before the Hungarian GP. That means the transfer is likely to be publicly confirmed between now and the race in Hungary. Watch the calendar.
facebook.image_caption: "Closing stages." Verstappen to McLaren. The exit clause triggers before Hungarian GP (July 24-26). The Piastri swap to Red Bull. Two weekends away. #F1

article.headline: Verstappen to McLaren: 'Closing Stages' as Exit Clause Mathematically Nears

stories.title: Alonso Sets Summer Break as His Deadline for 2027 F1 Decision

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: Alonso has set the summer break as his deadline for deciding on 2027. The Hungarian GP brings a Newey-designed aero revamp with major weight savings. Retire or continue into his 24th F1 season. His call. #F1 #AstonMartin

facebook.long_form:
Fernando Alonso has given himself a deadline.

The two-time world champion confirmed publicly Thursday that he will use the summer break to make his decision about 2027 — whether to continue into what would be his 24th Formula 1 season or bring one of the sport's longest careers to an end.

The context makes the timeline meaningful. Aston Martin arrives at the Hungarian Grand Prix (July 24-26) with what Alonso described as a "substantial" aerodynamic revamp — a package developed under Adrian Newey that brings significant weight savings alongside the aerodynamic changes. Alonso acknowledged the upgrade is real but added that it is "not the only thing on the table." The decision will be bigger than one race result.

On one side is Aston Martin's case for patience. The team has scored zero points in 2026 so far. Alonso has remained publicly committed to the project throughout a season that has given him nothing in return. The upgrade at Hungary is the first tangible evidence that Newey's work is translating into hardware.

On the other side is Alpine. Flavio Briatore is pushing actively to bring Alonso back to the team where he won his first championship in 2005. An incoming Gucci partnership adds commercial weight to that pitch.

Alonso at 44 remains one of the sharpest technical minds in the paddock. The upgrade at Hungary will tell him something. What it tells him will set the direction for 2027.
facebook.image_caption: Alonso set his own deadline: summer break. Aston Martin brings a Newey revamp to Hungarian GP. Alpine is pushing hard to bring him back. His 24th season — or his last. The clock is running.

article.headline: Alonso's F1 Future: Summer Break Is the Deadline for a Decision That Could End an Era

stories.title: Horner "Will Get Back" to F1 — FIA President Issues Statement; Newey Appears at Goodwood

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: FIA president on Christian Horner: "He will get back. Did he deliver? Do you want to win or not?" Meanwhile Newey drives a Red Bull he designed at Goodwood FOS this week — his parting gift car. #F1 #RedBull

facebook.long_form:
Two architects of Formula 1's most dominant era are back in the spotlight this week, in very different ways.

FIA president Mohammed Ben Sulayem made his position on Christian Horner's future in the sport clear on Thursday: "He will get back." The statement came with context: "Did he deliver? Do you want to win or not?" Ben Sulayem's words were not hedged. They reflected an institutional backing that carries weight across the paddock.

Horner attended the British Grand Prix as a guest of the FIA and FOM last weekend. It was his first paddock appearance since his exit from Red Bull. He has said publicly that he would only return to the sport for a team he believes can win. The FIA president's endorsement changes the calculus for any team willing to make a serious approach.

Meanwhile, Adrian Newey — now 67 and at Aston Martin — is making a surprise appearance at the Goodwood Festival of Speed this week (July 10-13). The occasion: he will drive a Red Bull car described as his "parting gift" project, making its public running debut. Newey spent decades designing the cars that defined Red Bull's championship years. Driving one at Goodwood as his farewell gesture is a fitting image for the end of an era.

Two men who shaped what Red Bull became. One of them may be returning to Formula 1. One of them is saying goodbye in the most graceful way possible.
facebook.image_caption: FIA president: "Horner will get back. Did he deliver? Do you want to win or not?" Meanwhile Newey drives a Red Bull he designed at Goodwood this week. The era they built together ends differently for each of them.

article.headline: FIA President on Horner: 'He Will Get Back' — The Statement That Changes the Conversation
